result:
If I could change the world in one way, it would be to dismantle the illusion of separateness—the deep-seated belief that we are fundamentally isolated individuals, tribes, or nations competing for survival.  

I would replace it with a widespread, felt understanding of interconnection: that our well-being is intrinsically tied to the well-being of others and the health of the planet. Not as a philosophical idea, but as a visceral reality that shapes decisions—from economics and politics to daily acts of kindness.  

This shift wouldn’t erase conflict or difference, but it would change the foundation from which we respond. Empathy would cease to be seen as soft or optional, but as essential as logic. Accountability would arise naturally from seeing how our actions ripple outward.  

The change wouldn’t be a new law or technology, but a quiet rewiring of human perception—a recognition that the boundary between “self” and “other” is more permeable than we’ve been taught.  

In that world, solving collective challenges—climate crisis, inequality, conflict—would no longer be thwarted by the question “What’s in it for me?” because the “me” would have expanded to include the whole.
